title: Frontend Hosting with S3 & CloudFront
content:
Frontend Hosting with S3 & CloudFront (OAC) In this step, you will set up static website hosting for the frontend application. We will use Amazon S3 to store the files and Amazon CloudFront to distribute them globally using Origin Access Control (OAC) to secure the bucket from direct public access.
1. Create S3 Bucket Open the Amazon S3 console. Select General purpose buckets on the left menu, then click Create bucket. Fill in the Create bucket form: Bucket name: Enter webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID (replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID with your actual AWS Account ID). Object Ownership: Select ACLs disabled / Bucket owner enforced (default). Block Public Access settings for this bucket: Check ✅ Block all public access (default). Bucket Versioning: Select Enable. Click Create bucket. 2. Create CloudFront Distribution with OAC Open the Amazon CloudFront console. Click Create distribution. Step 1 - Origin: Origin domain: Select the S3 bucket you just created (webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID.s3.amazonaws.com). Origin access: Select Origin access control settings (recommended). Click Create control setting: Keep name defaults and ensure signing behavior is set to Sign requests (recommended). Click Create. Step 2 - Default cache behavior: Viewer protocol policy: Select Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Allowed HTTP methods: Select GET, HEAD, OPTIONS. Step 3 - Settings: Default root object: Enter index.html. Click Create distribution. Once created, a warning banner will appear. Copy the generated S3 Bucket Policy text provided by CloudFront (you will paste this into S3 in the next step). 3. Configure S3 Bucket Policy Go back to your S3 bucket webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID in the S3 console. Select the Permissions tab. Scroll down to Bucket policy and click Edit. Paste the policy generated by CloudFront (which permits CloudFront to read objects from your bucket): { "Version": "2018-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "AllowCloudFrontOAC", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "s3:GetObject", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID/*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:distribution/YOUR_DISTRIBUTION_ID" } } } ] } (Ensure you replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID and YOUR_DISTRIBUTION_ID with your actual IDs). Click Save changes. 4. Configure Single Page Application (SPA) Routing To ensure client-side routing works correctly without throwing 403 Access Denied errors on page reloads:
Go back to your CloudFront distribution details. Select the Error pages tab. Click Create custom error response for two errors: Error 1: HTTP error code: Select 403: Forbidden. Customize error response: Select Yes. Response page path: Enter /index.html. HTTP response code: Select 200: OK. Click Create. Error 2: HTTP error code: Select 404: Not Found. Customize error response: Select Yes. Response page path: Enter /index.html. HTTP response code: Select 200: OK. Click Create. 5. Build and Deploy the Frontend Open your frontend source code folder on your local machine. Configure your environment file (e.g., .env or config.js) with the AWS values you saved: const config = { COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID: "us-east-1_xxxxxxxxx", COGNITO_APP_CLIENT_ID: "xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx", REST_API_ENDPOINT: "https://xxxxxxxxxx.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev", WEBSOCKET_API_ENDPOINT: "wss://xxxxxxxxxx.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev" }; Build the application static assets: npm run build Upload the built static files (found inside the dist/ or build/ directory) directly into your S3 bucket using the AWS S3 Console or the AWS CLI: aws s3 sync dist/ s3://webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID In the CloudFront distribution overview page, copy the Distribution Domain Name (e.g., https://xxxxxxxxxx.cloudfront.net) and access it in your browser to test!

title: CloudWatch Monitoring & Dashboard
content:
CloudWatch Monitoring & Dashboard Setup In this step, you will finalize the asynchronous event routing by setting up Amazon EventBridge Rules (which require target Lambda functions created in previous steps) and construct Amazon CloudWatch alarms and dashboards to monitor your application’s health.
1. Configure EventBridge Rules (Routing Events to Targets) Now that the target Lambdas are deployed, configure the EventBridge rules to automatically trigger them when specific events land on the bus.
1.1 Create Rule for AnswerSubmitted Open the Amazon EventBridge console. Select Rules on the left menu. Choose the event bus webquiz-dev-game-events from the dropdown. Click Create rule. Step 1 - Define rule detail: Name: webquiz-dev-answer-submitted Description: Trigger score calculator when answer is submitted. Event bus: webquiz-dev-game-events. Rule type: Select Rule with an event pattern. Click Next. Step 2 - Build event pattern: Event source: Select Other. Creation method: Select Custom pattern (JSON editor). Event pattern: { "source": ["quiz.game"], "detail-type": ["AnswerSubmitted"] } Click Next. Step 3 - Select targets: Target type: AWS service. Select a target: Lambda function. Function: Select webquiz-dev-score-calculator. Click Next. Step 4 - Review and create: Click Create rule. 1.2 Create Rule for GameEnded Under the webquiz-dev-game-events bus, click Create rule. Step 1 - Define rule detail: Name: webquiz-dev-game-ended. Event bus: webquiz-dev-game-events. Rule type: Rule with an event pattern. Step 2 - Build event pattern: Event source: Other. Event pattern: { "source": ["quiz.game"], "detail-type": ["GameEnded"] } Step 3 - Select targets: Target: Lambda function → Select webquiz-dev-game-results-saver. Click Create rule. 2. Configure CloudWatch Alarms Set up alerts to notify you of execution failures, excessive WebSocket connection loads, or database throttling.
Open the Amazon CloudWatch console. Select Alarms on the left menu, then click All alarms → Create alarm. 2.1 Lambda Errors Alarm Metric: Select AWS/Lambda → Errors → Sum. Period: 5 minutes. Threshold: Greater than 5. Name: webquiz-dev-lambda-errors. 2.2 WebSocket Connection Alarm Metric: Select AWS/ApiGateway → ConnectCount (Dimension: ApiId = your WebSocket API ID). Period: 1 minute. Threshold: Greater than 10000 (to track abnormal connection spike traffic). Name: webquiz-dev-websocket-connections. 2.3 DynamoDB Throttle Alarm Metric: Select AWS/DynamoDB → ThrottledRequests (Dimension: TableName = webquiz-dev-game-state). Period: 1 minute. Threshold: Greater than 1. Name: webquiz-dev-dynamodb-throttle. 3. Create CloudWatch Dashboard Build a unified operational dashboard to visualize metrics.
Select Dashboards on the left menu of the CloudWatch console. Click Create dashboard. Dashboard name: Enter webquiz-dev-dashboard. Add the following widgets: Widget 1 (Lambda Invocations): Metric widget showing Invocations for webquiz-dev-ws-message, webquiz-dev-score-calculator, and webquiz-dev-quiz-crud. Widget 2 (Lambda Durations): Metric widget showing Duration (p99 stat) for webquiz-dev-ws-message and webquiz-dev-score-calculator. Widget 3 (WebSocket Metrics): Metric widget showing ConnectCount and MessageCount for your WebSocket API. Widget 4 (DynamoDB Consumed Capacity): Metric widget showing ConsumedReadCapacityUnits, ConsumedWriteCapacityUnits, and ThrottledRequests for webquiz-dev-game-state. Click Save dashboard.

title: System Testing
content:
System Testing & Verification In this step, you will test and verify the entire end-to-end integration of the WebQuiz serverless application using API clients (Postman/curl) and terminal WebSocket clients (wscat).
1. Register and Log In (Get ID Token) To access the protected REST APIs, you need a Cognito JWT ID token.
Register a user: You can register a host user via the Cognito hosted UI or directly through the AWS CLI: aws cognito-idp sign-up \ --client-id YOUR_APP_CLIENT_ID \ --username host@test.com \ --password Password123! Confirm user registration: Confirm the user status in the Cognito User Pool console or via CLI: aws cognito-idp admin-confirm-sign-up \ --user-pool-id YOUR_USER_POOL_ID \ --username host@test.com Log in to get the JWT ID Token: Simulate login to receive the tokens: aws cognito-idp initiate-auth \ --client-id YOUR_APP_CLIENT_ID \ --auth-flow USER_SRP_AUTH \ --auth-parameters USERNAME=host@test.com,PASSWORD=Password123! Record the IdToken from the JSON response. You will use this token in the next step’s Authorization header. 2. Create a Quiz (REST HTTP API) Open Postman (or use curl) to send a request to your HTTP API Gateway endpoint:
Request Details: Method: POST URL: https://YOUR_HTTP_API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev/quizzes Headers: Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ID_TOKEN Content-Type: application/json Body (JSON): { "title": "AWS Serverless Trivia", "description": "Test your serverless knowledge", "settings": { "timeLimit": 20 } } Send the request. You should receive a 201 Created status returning a generated quizId. 3. Create a Game Room (REST HTTP API) Initialize a live quiz game session:
Request Details: Method: POST URL: https://YOUR_HTTP_API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev/rooms Headers: Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ID_TOKEN Body (JSON): { "quizId": "YOUR_QUIZ_ID" } Send the request. You should receive a 201 Created response containing a generated 6-digit roomPin (e.g., 582914). 4. Players Join the Room (Public REST API) Request Details: Method: POST URL: https://YOUR_HTTP_API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev/rooms/582914/join (replace 582914 with your active room PIN). Headers: Content-Type: application/json Body (JSON): { "nickname": "Alice" } Send the request. You will receive a 200 OK response with a player-specific orderId (e.g., player_172900_abc). 5. Establish Real-Time WebSocket Connections Now, simulate the live gameplay using two terminal windows.
5.1 Host Connection (Terminal 1) Open your terminal and connect as the game host:
wscat -c "wss://YOUR_WS_API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev?roomPin=582914&role=host" 5.2 Player Connection (Terminal 2) Open another terminal window and connect as Alice (the player):
wscat -c "wss://YOUR_WS_API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev?roomPin=582914&orderId=YOUR_PLAYER_ORDER_ID&role=player" 6. Simulate Gameplay Loop Execute the game state flow:
Host Starts Game: In Terminal 1 (Host), type and send: {"action": "START_GAME"} Verify that Terminal 2 (Player) immediately receives a GAME_STARTED payload message. Host Displays Question: In Terminal 1 (Host), send: {"action": "NEXT_QUESTION"} Verify that Terminal 2 receives a NEXT_QUESTION payload. Player Submits Answer: In Terminal 2 (Player), submit an answer before time expires: { "action": "SUBMIT_ANSWER", "data": { "questionId": "your-question-id", "answer": "A", "timeRemaining": 15 } } Verify that the Player receives a SCORE_UPDATE response indicating whether the answer was correct and their updated score. Host Ends Game: In Terminal 1 (Host), type and send: {"action": "END_GAME"} Verify that both terminals receive a GAME_ENDED message containing the game leaderboard. 7. Verify Game Results in Database Open the Amazon DynamoDB console:
Select the webquiz-dev-game-results table. Click Explore table items. Verify that a record exists representing Alice’s nickname, score, total correct answers, and final ranking.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
Resource Cleanup To avoid incurring unexpected charges on your AWS account, make sure to delete all the resources created during this workshop.
Follow this sequence to safely clean up your environment:
1. Delete Frontend Assets 1.1 Delete S3 Bucket Objects Open the Amazon S3 console. Select your bucket webquiz-dev-frontend-YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID. Click Empty to delete all objects inside the bucket. Once the bucket is empty, click Delete, type the name of the bucket to confirm, and delete it. 1.2 Delete CloudFront Distribution Open the Amazon CloudFront console. Locate your distribution, select it, and click Disable. Wait for the status to change to Disabled (a few minutes). Select the distribution again and click Delete. 2. Delete API Gateway Endpoints Open the Amazon API Gateway console. Locate the HTTP API webquiz-dev-http-api, click Actions (or click the three dots) → select Delete. Locate the WebSocket API webquiz-dev-websocket-api, click Actions → select Delete. 3. Delete Lambda Functions & IAM Permissions Open the AWS Lambda console. Under Functions, search for functions beginning with webquiz-dev-. Select all 7 functions: webquiz-dev-quiz-crud webquiz-dev-room-management webquiz-dev-ws-connect webquiz-dev-ws-disconnect webquiz-dev-ws-message webquiz-dev-score-calculator webquiz-dev-game-results-saver Click Actions → Delete. Open the Amazon IAM console: Under Roles, search for webquiz-dev-lambda-role and delete it. 4. Delete Database & Event Messaging Layer Open the Amazon DynamoDB console. Under Tables, select all 7 tables starting with webquiz-dev-: webquiz-dev-users webquiz-dev-quizzes webquiz-dev-questions webquiz-dev-rooms webquiz-dev-connections webquiz-dev-game-state webquiz-dev-game-results Click Delete table, type delete to confirm, and click Delete. Open the Amazon EventBridge console: Select Event buses, click on your bus webquiz-dev-game-events and delete it. 5. Delete Cognito User Pool Open the Amazon Cognito console. Locate and select your User Pool webquiz-dev-users. Click Delete, type the User Pool ID to confirm, and delete it.
